title: UAE scraps local healthcare barriers to launch single national insurance system
summary: Under the new system, Emiratis will be able to access healthcare services anywhere in the country regardless of their emirate of residence.
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
By Mark Adams, CEO of Clemenceau Medical Center – Dubai

UAE—President Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan has approved a unified national healthcare and health insurance system that will provide comprehensive medical coverage for Emirati citizens across all seven emirates.

The reform marks one of the most significant changes to the UAE’s healthcare landscape in recent years, removing long-standing barriers created by separate emirate-level insurance schemes and creating a single national framework for care.

Under the new system, Emiratis will be able to access healthcare services anywhere in the country regardless of their emirate of residence.

Previously, healthcare coverage was largely tied to local programmes such as Thiqa in Abu Dhabi, Saada in Sharjah, and other emirate-specific schemes.

As a result, patients seeking specialised treatment outside their home emirate often faced administrative challenges, coverage limitations, or lengthy travel to facilities approved by their insurance providers.

Expanding access to care nationwide

The unified model is designed to ensure that citizens receive the same standard of healthcare across the UAE.

A resident in Fujairah, for example, will be able to access specialist services in Abu Dhabi, Dubai, or any other emirate without navigating multiple insurance systems.

According to Ahmed Al Sayegh, Minister of Health and Prevention, the initiative reflects a national vision that places health and quality of life at the centre of the country’s development agenda.

The government has also described the system as a key step toward guaranteeing universal access to high-quality healthcare services while strengthening the resilience of the national health sector.

Healthcare leaders have welcomed the move, noting that it removes geographical barriers and improves access to centres of excellence, specialised treatments, and advanced medical facilities throughout the country.

Building a more connected healthcare system

Beyond improving access, the reform lays the foundation for a more integrated healthcare ecosystem.

Government officials have indicated that the new framework will support interconnected digital health platforms, nationwide medical records, and improved data sharing among healthcare providers.

These changes are expected to reduce duplication of services, improve resource utilisation, and streamline patient care.

The initiative aligns with broader efforts to modernise the UAE’s healthcare infrastructure through digital transformation.

Recent government initiatives have also focused on creating unified digital healthcare platforms that support workforce mobility, regulatory efficiency, and better coordination among federal and local health authorities.

A unified data infrastructure could also support the transition toward value-based healthcare models, where providers focus on improving patient outcomes rather than simply increasing service volumes.

By enabling healthcare organisations to monitor patient journeys across different facilities and emirates, the system creates opportunities for more coordinated and outcome-driven care.

Greater focus on Prevention and Population Health

Preventive healthcare is expected to play a central role in the new framework.

The UAE continues to face a growing burden of chronic diseases, including diabetes and cardiovascular conditions, both of which contribute significantly to healthcare spending and mortality.

A nationwide healthcare system gives authorities the ability to implement large-scale screening programmes, track population health trends more effectively, and identify individuals who may benefit from early interventions.

It also enables healthcare providers to move beyond traditional treatment models and adopt more proactive approaches focused on disease prevention and long-term health management.

This shift could strengthen community-based health programmes, improve chronic disease monitoring, and encourage earlier engagement with patients before symptoms become severe.

Supporting Inclusive healthcare

Alongside the healthcare reform, the Ministry of Health and Prevention has introduced a national policy framework for People of Determination.

The framework promotes a continuous care pathway that begins with early detection and extends through treatment, rehabilitation, and community support services.

Importantly, the policy also incorporates mental health services, recognising that overall wellbeing extends beyond physical health.

The approach seeks to ensure that people with disabilities, chronic illnesses, cognitive differences, and complex healthcare needs receive coordinated and accessible support throughout their lives.

Healthcare providers are expected to strengthen accessibility measures, enhance staff training, and develop patient-centred care pathways that encourage active participation in healthcare decisions.

New expectations for public and private providers

The reform also seeks to deepen collaboration between public and private healthcare organisations.

As the system moves toward greater integration, providers will be expected to align with common data standards, quality benchmarks, and national healthcare priorities.

This will require continued investment in digital infrastructure, interoperable health information systems, preventive health programmes, and inclusive care models.

The government believes these measures will help create a more coordinated healthcare environment capable of delivering consistent care across the country.

A structured national rollout

Unlike many policy announcements that remain broad in scope, the UAE has already identified the core components of implementation.

 Existing emirate-level insurance schemes will be integrated into a single national structure, while shared digital infrastructure will provide greater visibility into healthcare outcomes, service utilisation, and coverage gaps.

The unified healthcare and insurance system forms part of the UAE’s wider strategy to build a preventive, digitally connected, and sustainable healthcare sector.

By bringing together insurance coverage, healthcare providers, and national health data under one framework, the government aims to create a more efficient system that delivers equitable access to care for citizens across all seven emirates.
